2026-04-14T10:39:42.9393425Z [36;1m# Mask immediately to prevent timing vulnerabilities[0m
2026-04-14T10:39:42.9393973Z [36;1mAPI_KEY=$(openssl rand -base64 45 | tr -d '/+=')[0m

2026-04-14T10:51:29.3985568Z Now I have a complete root cause analysis. Let me create the GitHub issue.
2026-04-14T10:51:29.3986438Z 
2026-04-14T10:51:30.2998255Z ● create_issue (MCP: safeoutputs) · title: "CI failures on main: broken pnpm-lock.yaml causes wides…
2026-04-14T10:51:30.2999094Z   └ {"result":"success"}
2026-04-14T10:51:30.2999331Z 
2026-04-14T10:51:36.9447323Z The issue has been created. Here's a summary of my findings:
2026-04-14T10:51:36.9447808Z 
2026-04-14T10:51:37.9739235Z **Root Cause:** The automatic pnpm update commit (`a6df5fe`) bumped vitest from `4.1.2` → `4.1.4` across 421 packages and updated the lockfile snapshots section, but missed updating the `sdk/postgresql/postgresql-auth` importer section. This leaves the lockfile in an inconsistent state — it references a `4.1.2` snapshot that no longer exists.
2026-04-14T10:51:37.9740669Z 
2026-04-14T10:51:39.0886625Z **Impact:** Because every CI pipeline runs `pnpm install` for the whole workspace (no per-package filter), this single stale entry causes **all** CI pipelines to fail at the install step before any tests run, producing the generic "Bash exited with code '1'" annotations seen across 186+ failing check runs.
2026-04-14T10:51:39.0888101Z 
2026-04-14T10:51:40.5554455Z **Fix:** Change two lines in `pnpm-lock.yaml` in the `sdk/postgresql/postgresql-auth` section: replace `4.1.2(vitest@4.1.2)` → `4.1.4(vitest@4.1.4)` and update the full vitest peer-deps string from `4.1.2` references to `4.1.4`. The `4.1.4` snapshots already exist in the file.
2026-04-14T10:51:40.5555327Z 
2026-04-14T10:51:40.5640397Z 
2026-04-14T10:51:40.5641024Z Total usage est:        1 Premium request
2026-04-14T10:51:40.5641455Z API time spent:         8m 9s
2026-04-14T10:51:40.5641695Z Total session time:     11m 35s

2026-04-14T10:51:44.3600812Z

2026-04-14T10:51:44.3601380Z

2026-04-14T10:51:44.3602171Z 
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3602592Z ✓ $ cd /home/runner/work/azure-sdk-for-js/azure-sdk-for-js && grep -A50 "testing:" pnpm-workspace.yaml | grep "vitest\|coverage"
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3603018Z 
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3603717Z ◆ The workspace catalog specifies `^4.1.2` for vitest packages. When the pnpm update ran, it likely updated most of the lockfile to use `4.1.4` but missed the `postgresql-auth` package. So the lockfile has a mix:
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3604593Z   - Most packages: `4.1.4` in snapshots
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3605026Z   - `postgresql-auth`: still references `4.1.2` in its section but there's no `4.1.2` entry in the snapshots
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3605485Z   
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3606129Z   This is the broken lockfile issue! The pnpm update commit (`a6df5fe2`) updated most packages to vitest `4.1.4` but didn't update all packages, creating an inconsistency in the lockfile.
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3606803Z   
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3606954Z   
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3608473Z   Now I'm connecting this to the CI failures—if CI runs with `--frozen-lockfile`, it would fail when trying to resolve the missing `4.1.2` snapshot. But since each service's CI builds independently, the install might partially succeed or fail depending on whether pnpm can resolve the broken reference, which could explain why we're seeing inconsistent "Bash exited with code '1'" errors across different packages.
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3609912Z   
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3611391Z   Actually, the key insight is that the pnpm lockfile is workspace-wide, so a broken entry anywhere in it would cause `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` to fail for every package in the workspace—that's why we're seeing failures across seemingly unrelated services like `notificationhubs`, `search`, `iot`, and others. Let me trace back what the "Bash exited with code '1'" error maps to in the CI pipeline.

This broken lockfile is why `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` fails across all CI pipelines, even though the failures appear in different packages—they're all hitting the same shared workspace lockfile issue.

2026-04-14T10:51:44.3653050Z   
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3654727Z   So regular `pnpm install` should be failing due to the broken lockfile. The error message suggests running `pnpm install --no-frozen-lockfile`, which implies that regular `pnpm install` behaves like `--frozen-lockfile` by default. In pnpm v10, the lockfile is validated when present, and the `ERR_PNPM_LOCKFILE_MISSING_DEPENDENCY` error occurs when the package section references a version that's not in the snapshots section. 2026-04-14T10:51:44.3714070Z

2026-04-14T10:51:44.3714587Z 
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3714897Z ✓ safeoutputs-create_issue(title: CI failures on main: broken pnpm-lock.ya..., body: ## Summary
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3715244Z 
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3715354Z A broken `pnpm-lock.yaml` in..., labels: [bug])
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3715562Z 
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3715761Z ◆ The issue has been created. Here's a summary of my findings:
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3716047Z   
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3717330Z   **Root Cause:** The automatic pnpm update commit (`a6df5fe`) bumped vitest from `4.1.2` → `4.1.4` across 421 packages and updated the lockfile snapshots section, but missed updating the `sdk/postgresql/postgresql-auth` importer section. This leaves the lockfile in an inconsistent state — it references a `4.1.2` snapshot that no longer exists.
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3718466Z   
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3719413Z   **Impact:** Because every CI pipeline runs `pnpm install` for the whole workspace (no per-package filter), this single stale entry causes **all** CI pipelines to fail at the install step before any tests run, producing the generic "Bash exited with code '1'" annotations seen across 186+ failing check runs.
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3720442Z   
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3721546Z   **Fix:** Change two lines in `pnpm-lock.yaml` in the `sdk/postgresql/postgresql-auth` section: replace `4.1.2(vitest@4.1.2)` → `4.1.4(vitest@4.1.4)` and update the full vitest peer-deps string from `4.1.2` references to `4.1.4`. The `4.1.4` snapshots already exist in the file.
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3722373Z 
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3722444Z Statistics:
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3722612Z   Turns: 52
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3722784Z   Tools: 54/54 succeeded
2026-04-14T10:51:44.3723023Z   Tokens: 3,110,479 total (3,087,608 in / 22,871 out)

to specify what pull request data needs to be retrieved from GitHub. \nPossible options: \n 1. get - Get details of a specific pull request.\n 2. get_diff - Get the diff of a pull request.\n 3. get_status - Get combined commit status of a head commit in a pull request.\n 4. get_files - Get the list of files changed in a pull request. Use with pagination parameters to control the number of results returned.\n 5. get_review_comments - Get review threads on a pull request. Each thread contains logically grouped review comments made on the same code location during pull request reviews. Returns threads with metadata (isResolved, isOutdated, isCollapsed) and their associated comments. Use cursor-based pagination (perPage, after) to control results.\n 6. get_reviews - Get the reviews on a pull request. When asked for review comments, use get_review_comments method.\n 7. get_comments - Get comments on a pull request. Use this if user doesn't specifically want review comments. Use with pagination parameters to control the number of results returned.\n 8. get_check_runs - Get check runs for the head commit of a pull request. \\u003craychen@microsoft.com\\u003e\",\"author\":{\"name\":\"Azure SDK migrate to #platform/* subpath imports (#37974)\\n\\n## Migrate `@typespec/ts-http-runtime` to `#platform/*` subpath imports\\n\\nReplaces warp's `polyfillSuffix` mechanism with Node.js [subpath\\nimports](https://nodejs.org/api/packages.html#subpath-imports)\\n(`#platform/*`) for all platform-specific code in `ts-http-runtime`.\\n\\n### Why\\n\\n`polyfillSuffix` works by silently swapping files at build time (e.g.\\n`foo.ts` → `foo-browser.mts`) based on the warp target. This is\\ninvisible to TypeScript — all platform variants share one set of type\\ndeclarations, so the compiler can't distinguish what's available on each\\ntarget. It also forces runtime environment sniffing for any logic that\\nneeds to know its platform, which is fundamentally unreliable in hybrid\\nenvironments.\\n\\nNode.js subpath imports solve both problems: each platform variant gets\\nits own types (caught at compile time), and the correct module is\\nselected by the package manager/bundler resolving `package.json`\\nconditions — no runtime guesswork.\\n\\nConcrete benefits:\\n\\n- **Type safety** — each platform variant is independently type-checked.\\nFor example, the Node `isNodeReadableStream` returns `x is Readable`\\n(from `node:stream`), the browser version uses `instanceof\\nReadableStream`, and RN returns `x is never`. The old approach silently\\nproduced incorrect type narrowing on non-Node platforms.\\n\\n- **Environment detection correctness** — the old `checkEnvironment.ts`\\nran `typeof window`, `typeof process`, etc. on every platform, producing\\nwrong results in Electron, Cloudflare Workers, Deno, and React Native\\n(see [linked issues](#runtime-detection-issues) below). Per-platform\\n`env` modules use static constants that are compile-time truths.\\n\\n- **Simpler testing** — the old test scripts required `dev-tool run\\nbuild-package` and `dev-tool run build-test` to manuall
2026-04-14T10:51:44.5742156Z ...
